For h c < h the fish are hunted to extinction (assuming that the fish population can’t become negative). 3.7.5 a) g ˙   =   d g d t   =   k 1 s 0   −   k 2 g   +   k 3 g 2 k 4 2   +   g 2 k 4 k 3   d g k 4 d t   =   k 1 s 0 k 3   −   k 2 k 4 k 3   g k 4   +   g 2 k 4 2 1   +   g 2 k 4 2 x   =   g k 4   s   =   k 1 s 0 k 3   r   =   k 2 k 4 k 3   τ   =   k 3 k 4   t d x d τ   =   s   −   r x   +   x 2 1   +   x 2 b) If s = 0 d x d τ   =   0   −   r x   +   x 2 1   +   x 2   =   0 x   =   0 ,   1   ±   1   −   4 r 2 2 r And there are three real fixed points, two of which are positive since the square root is at most one, if r   <   r c   =   1 2 . c) g (0) = 0 corresponds to x (0) = 0, and increasing the parameter s translates the graph vertically. There is only one fixed point at x = 0 for 1 2   <   r since the graph is strictly decreasing, and x will stay close to the fixed point as s is increased. At r   ≤   1 2 one, two, and three fixed points are possible with two saddle-node bifurcations occurring as s increases. Starting with s = 0, increasing s , and then returning to s = 0: If 1 2   <   r , the system will go towards the x = 0 stable fixed point since that is the only fixed point. If r   =   1 2 , the system can go back to the x = 0 fixed point or the stable half of the semistable fixed point depending on how fast s changes, but noise in a real system would most likely bump x to the unstable side of the semistable fixed point and then the system would go towards the x = 0 fixed point. If r   <   1 2 , the system will almost surely be near the far right stable fixed point, despite s returning to its original value. d) The saddle-node bifurcations will occur when d x d τ   =   0 and d d x   d x d τ   =   0 , which gives us two equations to solve for r and s in terms of x . d d x   ( s   −   r x   +   x 2 1   +   x 2 )   =   − r   +   2 x ( 1   +   x 2 ) 2   =   0 r   =   2 x ( 1   +   x 2 ) 2 s   −   r x   +   x 2 1   +   x 2   =   s   −   2 x ( 1   +   x 2 ) 2 x   +   x 2 1   +   x 2   =   0 s   =   x 2 ( 1   −   x 2 ) ( x 2   +   1 ) 2 These are saddle-node bifurcations. e) 3.7.7 a) lim n → ∞   β p n K n   +   p n   =   lim n → ∞   β p n p n K n p n   +   p n p n   =   lim n → ∞   β K n p n   +   1   =   β H   ( p   −   K ) where H ( p ) is the Heaviside step function. b) c) We’ll assume α > 0 and β > 0 due to being biological measurements, as well as δK > β from the problem statement.
